chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee welcomes colleagues and Commissioner Nadia Shahada to the FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for the NYC Department of Investigation, noting co-chair Krishnan and attending council members.
chapters.title: Chair Lee welcomes attendees to DOI FY27 budget hearing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Ah, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Welcome to the final FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for the day on New York City Department of Investigation, Councilmember Linda Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm pleased to be joined by my colleague, Councilmember Shekar Krishnan, Chair of the Committee on Oversight and Investigations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have been joined by Councilmembers Ung, Brewer,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: and Riley.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is there anyone on Zoom?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I don't think so.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So welcome, Commissioner Nadia Shahada and your team, and thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

chapters.summary: Lee presents DOI's proposed FY27 budget of $53.7 million, a 0.7% decrease from the preliminary plan, and raises concerns about an 11-position vacancy reduction on an already diminished headcount.
chapters.title: Chair Lee presents DOI FY27 budget figures and headcount reduction concerns

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY26-30 with a proposed fiscal 27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The New York City

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Department of Investigations proposed fiscal 27 budget of $53.7 million represents 0.04% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This is a decrease of $385,066 or 0.7% from the $54.1 million originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This decrease results from several actions, primarily the reduction of vacant positions and unspent salaries.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 26, DOIs headcount was one less than their headcount at the end of December 2025.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: An 11 position vacancy reduction has been levied onto a department that has lost substantial headcount and resources over the last few fiscal years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Today, we look forward to discussing the impact that this will have on the department's operations and how we can work together to explore ways to restore the resources the department needs to carry out its responsibilities.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Council Member and Chair Krishnan, for his opening statement.

chapters.summary: Chair Krishnan emphasizes DOI's vital role in rooting out corruption, protecting New Yorkers from greedy corporations and bad landlords, and restoring trust in government without fear or favor.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on DOI's anti-corruption mission and council partnership

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Good afternoon and welcome to the FY 2027 Executive Budget Hearing for the Committee on Oversight Investigations.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Shekar Krishnan, Chair of the Committee on Oversight Investigations.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I want to thank Finance Chair Councilmember Linda Lee for joining me today, along with all my colleagues who are here in reviewing the Department of Investigations FY 27 Executive Budget to understand the needs of this crucial department and the role the staff play in oversight of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Commissioner, thank you so much for your testimony today and for the whole DOI team who is here as well and for your work every single day to ensure that we root out corruption and fraud in our city government and ensure that it's performing to its highest standards.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I see this committee and its partnership with the Department of Investigation to be a powerful tool in rooting out corruption and a way to hold anyone or anything accountable that takes advantage of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: DOI is vital to this effort, vital to ensuring we can protect New Yorkers from greedy corporations, bad landlords, and to root out corruption in city government.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Together, we are fighting to restore trust in our government and prove to New Yorkers that our city fights for you and serves you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Without fear and without favor.

chapters.summary: Krishnan criticizes former Mayor Adams for slashing DOI's budget and workforce by 28% while the agency was actively investigating corruption at the highest levels of City Hall, including the federal indictment of Adams.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on Adams administration corruption and 28% DOI workforce cuts

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Over the years of the Eric Adams administration, we saw troubling amounts of corruption and misuse of city resources.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And while DOI and the FBI investigated Adams on a range of accusations of bribery, conspiracy, and fraud,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And while the work of DOI leading to the federal indictment that we saw was a crucial part of that effort, former Mayor Adams slashed the budget for the Department of Investigations.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We have to be very honest about that.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: At a time when an agency was investigating corruption at the highest levels of City Hall, City Hall was depriving that very agency of the resources it needed to do its job.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Yet it did it anyway, and it did it very effectively.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: With the limited resources that DOI was saddled with,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: They continued and still continue to uphold their duty to hold government accountable.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: But make no mistake, Adams severely cut the workforce of DOI and tried to impair their ability to properly and comprehensively oversee city agencies and root out corruption.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: 28% of the DOI workforce was slashed.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: 28% and millions of dollars were taken away from the agency.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I deeply believe that this agency needs the funding to be able to do its job and to be the important independent arm of government that they are.

chapters.summary: Krishnan expresses concern that the executive budget still requires workforce reductions and over-relies on asset forfeiture funds from a years-old CityTime case that will run dry in approximately two years, arguing DOI needs more baseline resources.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on executive budget concerns and forfeiture fund over-reliance

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I remain concerned that the executive budget continues to require workforce reductions for vital positions and an over-reliance on asset forfeiture funds of a years-old city time case that will run dry, most likely in two years.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It is crucial that this city budget

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: brings in more funding and resources for DOI.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: to correct from the years from which this agency saw a reduction in the workforce and its funding.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Point blank, DOI needs more resources and funding to operate, to do its job, and to fulfill its sacred independent mission in city government.

chapters.summary: Krishnan calls for an additional $4.5 million for DOI's 9/11 toxins investigation to bring accountability for what the Giuliani administration knew about air quality after the World Trade Center attacks, and urges restoring DOI's headcount and funding.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan calls for $4.5M for 9/11 toxins investigation and restoring DOI headcount

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Additionally, this morning, I joined Speaker Menin and Councilmember Brewer in calling for an additional $4.5 million

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: for DOI for the 9-11 investigation so that they have the resources they need to bring accountability and answers

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: to what the Julian administration knew about the air quality and presence of toxins following the horrific attack on the World Trade Center on September 11, 2001.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Having this funding in place will enable DOI to complete its investigation as to the knowledge of city government when it came to toxins in the air and will enable DOI to have the resources to release all the documents in regards to this investigation.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We need to restore the department's headcount and funding so that it can continue to provide comprehensive oversight without triaging, without pulling from other staff, without scrambling for resources.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: An agency with the critical mission that this one has needs the tools to do its job.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing about DOI's budget requests and working together to ensure a fully funded agency.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I know the Mamdani administration and City Hall share this goal, and I look forward to seeing it.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: forward to our council and city hall working together to making sure our final budget delivers on increased resources for this agency.

chapters.summary: Krishnan thanks committee staff including financial analyst Owen Katowski, policy analysts Erica Cohen and Alex Yablon, committee counsel Nicole Kata, and his own staff, then welcomes Commissioner Shahada before passing back to Chair Lee.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan thanks committee staff and welcomes Commissioner

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I want to thank our Oversight Investigations Committee staff for their hard work, our financial analyst Owen Katowski, policy analyst Erica Cohen and Alex Yablon, committee counsel Nicole Kata, and the incredible Oversight Investigations Division.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I also want to thank my staff, Shanel Martinez, Victoria Opperman, and Hunter DeVine-Gracia.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I would like to welcome, of course, again, Commissioner Shahada and our leadership team here today.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I'm now going to pass it back to Chair Lee.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee announces public testimony on Wednesday, June 10th at 9:30 a.m., then committee counsel Brian Sarfo administers the oath to Commissioner Shahada before testimony begins.
chapters.title: Chair Lee announces public testimony date and administers oath

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just a couple housekeeping items before we start.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So because of the shortened timeline for our budget hearings, we are going to have public testimony on one day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please feel free to join us.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It should be fun.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We'll try to make it as lively as possible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So Wednesday, June 10th, starting at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Wednesday, June 10th, starting at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you guys know anyone in the public that would like to sign up to testify, please make sure they do so.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, actually, I'll pass it off to Brian Sarfo, our amazing committee counsel, to administer the oath.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions, Commissioner Shahada?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think the light, is the light on?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Does it turn red?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you, Sergeant.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Nadia Shahata introduces herself in her sixth week as DOI Commissioner, describes DOI's mission to prevent and expose corruption, and praises the dedication of DOI's investigative, legal, and operational staff.
chapters.title: Commissioner Shihata introduces herself and outlines DOI's mission

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Lee and the Committee on Finance and Chair Krishnan and the Committee on Oversight and Investigations.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: My name is Nadia Shahata and I'm the Commissioner of the New York City Department of Investigation.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I'm honored to present this important testimony on DOI's fiscal year 2027 executive plan as I mark my sixth week as DOI Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It has been an exhilarating and informative time for me as I meet the impressive DOI staff, connect with the agency's prosecutorial partners, and delve into the important work that is ongoing at the agency.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: As an investigative fact-finding agency, DOI's mission is to prevent and expose corruption, fraud, and other wrongdoing that undermines the city,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: depletes precious public resources, and compromises the faith and trust of New Yorkers in their municipal government.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The agency is a powerhouse, conducting both civil and criminal investigations, working with prosecutors to hold wrongdoers accountable, and issuing recommendations for reforms that strengthen city government operations.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The heart of the agency is DOI's extremely dedicated staff.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It's administrative and operational personnel, investigators, attorneys, auditors, IT specialists, and so many more.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In my brief time at DOI so far, I have been inspired by their dedication and

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: extremely hard work to uphold integrity and protect the city from the corrosive impact of corruption.

chapters.summary: Shahada reports that the proposed FY27 budget of $53.7 million is insufficient, with headcount dropping from 588 in FY2020 to 444 in FY2027—a loss of 144 positions. She describes constructive discussions with the Mayor's Chief Counsel and OMB Director about DOI's budget crisis.
chapters.title: Commissioner on DOI's dire budget situation and headcount decline since FY2020

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: When I arrived in late April, it was immediately clear that my priority needed to be digging into the agency's dire budget situation.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Let's try another one.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: My assessment is that the funding proposed for DOI in the fiscal year 2027 executive plan is not enough to sustain DOI's current operations.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The proposed $53.7 million budget is $2.1 million less than the fiscal year 2026 adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And approximately $12.8 million less in baseline funding compared to fiscal year 2020, reflecting over $8.3 million in PS baseline reductions.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Due to years of successive budget cuts, virtually all investigative and operational units across the agency

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: remain severely understaffed.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI's budgeted headcount, including city tax levy and intra-city funded staff, as well as employees on loan from other agencies,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Has dropped from 588 staff in fiscal year 2020 to 444 in fiscal year 2027,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: resulting in 144 fewer DOI positions available to fight corruption, waste, and inefficiency in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I have met with the Mayor's Chief Counsel and the Director of the Office of Management and Budget to outline these stark realities, and I am grateful that they took the time to listen and understand how the budget deficiencies are impacting DOI.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It is not an overstatement to describe this current moment as pivotal for the agency.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I am pleased to report that these discussions have been very constructive and encouraging.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I am confident that City Hall shares our view on the vital importance of DOI's mandate and understands our current budget situation, including our need for additional funding to stabilize DOI

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: and ensure the agency can continue to fulfill its crucial oversight mandate.

chapters.summary: Shahada details DOI's need for approximately $6 million to stabilize operations, including $4.53M for OTPS restoration, $1.14M to reverse the 50% vacancy reduction, and $290K for five entry-level investigators, plus $4 million for the council-mandated 9/11 toxins investigation using a third-party vendor.
chapters.title: Commissioner details $6M stabilization and $4M WTC investigation funding needs

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I hope and expect that City Hall and the City Council will properly address our need for additional funding in the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: As city officials continue to weigh DOI's budgetary concerns and decide how best to address them,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I want to be transparent with the council and the public on the budgetary landscape faced by DOI with the current cuts set forth in the executive plan, as I have been with the mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI will need approximately $6 million to stabilize its budget for the

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: the next fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: This includes approximately $4.53 million to restore other than personnel services or OTPS funds that have been cut in prior years, approximately $1.14 million to reverse the recent 50%

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: vacancy reduction that was imposed across the board for all city agencies, regardless of size or need in the executive plan,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: and $290,520 to fund five new entry-level investigator positions to sustain DOI's core operations.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In addition, DOI requires an additional $4 million to conduct the investigation mandated by the City Council

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: regarding what prior mayoral administrations knew about environmental toxins produced by the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks on the World Trade Center.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: This funding will pay for a third-party vendor with specialized expertise to assist with this record's intensive investigation.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The vendor would report to and be managed by DOI.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: To be clear, if DOI does not receive additional funding, I will need to move forward with some very difficult decisions that will have a tangible impact on our operations and ability to meet our anti-corruption mandate.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Without the additional PS funding, DOI will need to eliminate defunded lines and key vacancies across critical areas that include executive leadership,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: legal oversight, frontline investigative squads, and vital technical support staff.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI will also need to decrease incoming salary levels for certain remaining vacancies, which also has an impact on the experience level and skill that DOI will be able to recruit and hire.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Recognizing the challenging budgetary landscape faced by the city, the funds I am requesting are strictly limited to providing stabilization funding for DOI to ensure it

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: can fulfill its essential operations and conduct the council-mandated World Trade Center investigation.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: At this time, I am not requesting further investments in the agency

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: to meaningfully enhance its anti-corruption capabilities.

chapters.summary: Shahada notes that prior DOI leadership raised the same budget alarms for years without success, that the agency is stretched to its limit, and that her funding request is strictly for stabilization—not enhancement—given her hope that City Hall will address the gap in the adopted budget.
chapters.title: Commissioner on prior leadership's budget warnings and limited funding request rationale

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: This limited funding request reflects my deep concern that further cuts, as contemplated in the executive budget, will impact DOI's core functions.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: This concern is not new.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Under its prior leadership,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI rang the alarm bell on its budget needs for years without avail.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Citywide budget reductions have stretched this small agency, which is already running a lean operation, to its limit.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Bluntly, the fiscal year 2027 executive budget does not provide the funding that DOI needs to adequately and

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: and fully meet its mission.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Given recent positive and constructive discussions I've had with City Hall, I am hopeful this will be rectified in the adopted budget.

chapters.summary: Shahada breaks down the $53.7M FY27 budget into $29.3M in personal services and $21.4M in city-funded OTPS, supplemented by $3M in forfeiture funds. She explains that only 275 DOI-funded positions remain after accounting for CCPC staff, with 162 additional intra-city and on-loan staff bringing the total to 437.
chapters.title: Commissioner breaks down DOI's FY27 budget: PS, OTPS, and headcount details

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: My testimony.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Testimony today will walk you through the proposed fiscal year 27 executive budget, the tangible impact of the proposed budget cuts, and the roles that forfeiture and integrity monitorships play in our budget and the associated challenges with each.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI's $53.7 million fiscal year 2027 executive budget includes $29.3 million

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: in personal services or PS funds, which primarily funds staff salaries, and $21.4 million in city-funded OTPS, which pays for DOI's lease obligations and integrity.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: monitorships.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: This OTPS funding will be supplemented with $3 million from DOI's forfeiture funds for a total OTPS spend of $24.4 million.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We have been supplementing our budget significantly with forfeiture funding, particularly for OTPS items.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Such as computers, laptops, vehicles, trainings, and office equipment.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I will describe our forfeiture funding in more detail shortly.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The current budgeted headcount for DOI-funded employees in fiscal year 2027 is only 282 positions.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Seven of those positions are staff

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: for an entirely separate entity, the Commission to Combat Police Corruption, that is not part of DOI, but which is nonetheless funded through DOI.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: This brings down the budgeted headcount for DOI

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I funded employees working on DOI matters to just 275 positions.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: An additional 162 interest city and on loan staff report to DOI for a total of 437 employees who work on DOI matters.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The fiscal year 2027 executive budget

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: also contains nearly $3.4 million for which DOI simply acts as a pass-through entity.

chapters.summary: Shahada explains that $3.4M of DOI's budget is pass-through funding for CCPC and a borough-based jails integrity monitor that doesn't support DOI operations. She describes space savings from relocating CCPC and renting a floor to OCA generating $1.8M annually, plus $6M in financial recoveries and $5M saved through the asylum seeker monitorship.
chapters.title: Commissioner on pass-through funds, space savings, and $6M in financial recoveries

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In other words, these funds do not support DOI's internal staff or operations in any way.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Specifically, $855,000 is allocated for the separate entity, CCPC,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: while the remaining $2.6 million funds an integrity monitor that reports to DOI and is monitoring the construction of the borough-based jails.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Despite these budget constraints, DOI has still sought savings as mandated by the mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In collaboration with the City Department of Citywide Administrative Services and OMB,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We assessed our space footprint as part of a citywide space savings initiative and relocated CCPC from leased space at 17 Battery Place to DOI headquarters at 180 Maiden Lane.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: This saved the city approximately $157,700 annually.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Separately, DOI rented one of its

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: floors at 180 Maiden Lane to the New York State Office of Court Administration, which generates approximately $1.8 million annually.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: in lease expense reimbursement to the city.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: However, OMB does not credit these lease savings towards the cuts it has mandated in DOI's budget.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: More importantly, so far in fiscal year 2026, DOI investigations have resulted in financial recoveries of more than $6 million that have been collected by the city.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: These recoveries are case dependent and DOI cannot predict from year to year how much those financial recoveries will be.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But in fiscal year 2026 so far, DOI's work has added $6 million to the city's general fund.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Those financial recoveries are on top of the public funds that have been protected as a result of our investigations and integrity monitorships, such as the $5.07 million in public funds saved as part of the Asylum Seeker monitorship.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That monitorship found an array of vendor invoicing errors that included mistaken double billing and overpayment of advance deposits and other anomalies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And we have looked for opportunities to develop new avenues to fight fraud and save the city money.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Last week, Corporation Counsel Steve Banks and I engaged in preliminary discussions on ways DOI and the law department

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: can work collaboratively to recoup city funds lost in non-criminal cases, including through civil fraud cases under the False Claims Act.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It is this sort of innovative thinking that sends a strong and unified message that the city as a whole is standing

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: up to address corruption and fraud on all available fronts.

chapters.summary: Shahada details $1.06M in one-time FY26 cuts and $1.14M in baseline FY27 PS cuts from the vacancy mandate, requiring DOI to defund nine funded vacancies including critical senior-level investigative and legal positions, and forgo prior new-needs requests for 14 vacant positions and 17 baseline positions.
chapters.title: Commissioner details executive budget PS cuts and vacancy reduction impacts

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In the executive budget, OMB has imposed $1.06 million in one-time cuts in FY 2026 and $1.14 million in baseline cuts starting in FY 2027.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In fiscal year 2026, the executive budget cuts $500,000 in PS funding for the vacancy reduction mandate, reducing DOI's budgeted headcount by 11 in the baseline.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It also cuts $550,000 in a one-time PS accrual as projected by OMB, as well as $15,691 in a one-time savings for heat, light, and power.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In fiscal year 2027, the executive budget cuts $1.14 million

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: in baseline PS funding for the vacancy mandate.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: To meet these cuts, DOI will have to defund and defer hiring for nine positions to satisfy the 50% vacancy reduction mandate, including critical senior level investigatory and legal positions.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: This action will further deplete investigative and operational squads across the agency.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI will also have to forgo essential staffing made as part of our prior new needs request to OMB, specifically the restoration of 14 vacant positions at $1.4 million.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI previously

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: utilize the funds for these positions to offset serious recruitment, hiring, and retention challenges caused by prior budget reductions and the city's two-for-one hiring limitation.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We will also need to forego a request for 17 baseline positions for investigative and operational staff that include data analysts, confidential and background investigators, and auditors, among others.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In fact, DOI has already reduced this request to five entry-level investigators in our most recent new needs request to OMB for the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In addition, DOI requested funding for the mandated World Trade Center toxins investigation in the November and January plans, which OMB deferred to the incoming administration's review.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: A request for $4 million for this investigation has been resubmitted for consideration in the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI cannot begin this council-mandated work until necessary funding is secured.

chapters.summary: Shahada explains DOJ rules governing forfeiture funds—they cannot fund permanent salaries and must supplement, not supplant, DOI's budget. She reports DOI has reprogrammed nearly $19M since FY2023 and plans $11M more in FY2026, but warns these finite funds will run out in 2-3 years and the city cannot legally base budget decisions on forfeiture availability.
chapters.title: Commissioner on forfeiture fund rules, reprogramming history, and sustainability concerns

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: With respect to forfeiture funds, federal and state law allow the profits of criminal activity to be forfeited to the government

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: and shared with the investigating agencies that worked on the case, with the general guideline that these funds must support law enforcement activities.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The majority of DOI's forfeiture funds are the result of partnering with federal prosecutors on criminal investigations.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And there are specific Department of Justice rules that dictate how forfeiture funds may and may not be spent.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: For instance, forfeiture funds may not be used to fund salaries for permanent staff positions, but may be used for training or law enforcement technology.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Specifically, the guidelines require that forfeiture funds supplement, not supplant, DOI's budget.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Importantly, the city is prohibited from making budgetary decisions based on the availability of DOI's federal forfeiture funds.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In other words, the city cannot decide to cut DOI's budget based on the amount of forfeiture funds DOI receives, with the expectation that forfeiture will cover the budgetary gap.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Most importantly, if the city were to consider federal forfeiture funds in determining DOI's budget, per DOJ guidelines, DOI could lose its access to the funding.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Over the last several years, DOI has reprogrammed federal forfeiture funds, all in accordance with DOJ guidelines, to manage mandated budget cuts from the city,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: specifically related to OTPS items such as updating the agency's computer infrastructure, including our case management system, as well as purchasing computers and laptops for agency staff.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Since fiscal year 2023, DOI has spent nearly $19 million by reprogramming its forfeiture dollars to supplement DOI's budget and support the agency's essential law enforcement operations.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In fiscal year 2026,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI plans to spend an additional $11 million by reprogramming its forfeiture funds.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: in accordance with DOJ guidelines.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But this type of reprogramming is not sustainable in the long term because forfeiture funds are finite and there is no way to predict how much forfeiture funding will come into DOI in the future from our investigations.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And again, it is not permissible for the city to make budgeting decisions

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: based on the availability of forfeiture funds.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Put simply, the city must start to properly fund DOI before these funds run out.

chapters.summary: Shahada requests $4.5M in OTPS restoration for critical technology needs including cybersecurity and data storage, then describes DOI's integrity monitorship program—rehabilitative monitorships paid by vendors and programmatic monitorships paid by the city—including the asylum seeker initiative and borough-based jail construction oversight.
chapters.title: Commissioner on OTPS restoration needs and integrity monitorship program

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: To reduce reliance on forfeiture funds, DOI has requested approximately $4.5 million in OTPS funding restoration in the adopted budget,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: to pay for core critical needs, such as computer software maintenance and subscriptions and maintenance of DOI's cybersecurity and data storage networks, which are technology items that should not be deferred.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI's integrity monitorship program is a cornerstone of the agency's anti-corruption work.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Integrity monitorships allow the city to have real-time oversight of vendors with integrity issues and of large-scale, high-dollar city projects.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The oversight is provided by outside integrity monitors that report to and are managed by DOI.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Rehabilitative monitorships permit the city to enter into or to continue contracts with companies that might otherwise be precluded from doing business with the city

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: due to identified integrity issues, but that city agencies have determined are necessary for continuity or provision of city services.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In these cases, vendors pay for the monitorships.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Programmatic monitorships proactively help the city to oversee large-scale city projects, such as the Asylum Seeker Initiative and the borough-based jail construction, with the goal of ensuring compliance with city regulations and realizing cost savings.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In these cases, the city pays for the integrity monitors.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The funds for these monitors are included as part of DOI's budget, but all of the money goes directly to the integrity monitors.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: None of it supports DOI staffing or resources.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Significantly, this funding artificially inflates DOI's budget, lending the false impression that it supports DOI's operations and staffing.

chapters.summary: Shahada advocates for budgetary independence for DOI, noting the agency proposed this reform to the City Commission to Strengthen Local Democracy and plans to submit it to the Mayor's new Committee on Government Efficiency. She argues independence would protect DOI from politically motivated retaliation and ensure adequate funding to oversee a $124.7 billion city budget.
chapters.title: Commissioner advocates for DOI budget independence and charter reform

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Today's testimony underscores the significance that budgetary independence would have on DOI.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In fact, DOI advocated for this reform, among others, before the City Commission to Strengthen Local Democracy, which compiled a host of prospective charter revisions, including budgetary independence for DOI.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI plans to submit the same proposal to the Mayor's Committee on Government Efficiency, which was just announced last week.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Funding DOI at an appropriate percentage of the city budget and giving DOI control over its hiring, for example, would safeguard DOI's independence and reinforce the city's commitment to prevent fraud, waste, and abuse.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Budget independence would reduce the risk of politically motivated retaliation against DOI or the appearance of such retaliation by reducing our funding.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And it would finally provide DOI with the financial resources to fully oversee a city with a $124.7 billion budget, more than 300,000 employees, and billions of dollars in capital construction.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In the meantime, DOI requests the city restore $6 million to its budget in order to maintain the agency's core operations and $4 million spread over two fiscal years to fund the World Trade Center investigation.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I want to thank the Mayor's Chief Counsel and the OMB Budget Director for hearing and understanding my concerns, and I am very thankful, as well as hopeful,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: that additional funding to stabilize DOI's ability to conduct its vital oversight mandate is under active discussion and consideration at City Hall.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I also want to thank council members for your focus on DOI's budget and for providing me the opportunity to walk you through the numbers.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I'm happy to answer any questions.

chapters.summary: Lee asks why DOI's savings target waiver was denied. Shahada confirms DOI applied but was not exempted, though the denial occurred before her tenure. Lee questions how to protect staffing given DOI's oversight role and revenue generation, and Shahada suggests charter reform for budget independence.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions DOI on savings waiver denial and protecting staffing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been also joined by Council Members Aviles, Carr, and Joang.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just out of curiosity, and I know this is something that Council Member Brewer had worked on also, but why did you try applying for the waiver?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Why did you not?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Get the waiver if you applied for it.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The waiver from the savings target?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We did.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We did apply for it.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We did.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, you did.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What was the reason that they said no?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I believe some of that happened before I joined DLI, so I'm not sure what reasoning was provided, but we were not exempted from it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Interesting.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, just given the cuts that have happened to the department and looking into the future, I'm just wondering how we can prevent this from happening because it is a city agency that

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: does robust oversight and also provides revenue for the city as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So this is why I'm just wondering if there's any ways that you're looking at to protect staffing from sort of this budget cut in the future.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Not budget cut, but yeah.

chapters.summary: Lee asks for examples of how DOI investigations save the city money. Shahada cites $6M in FY26 restitution collections, including $1.6M from a defendant who inflated fire alarm system invoices, and $5M in overbilling identified through the asylum seeker monitorship. Lee requests a follow-up list of examples and titles that accomplish savings.
chapters.title: Chair Lee asks for examples of DOI investigations saving city money

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That is why we would welcome changes to the city charter that made our budget truly independent because this concern happens every year and given the important oversight mandate that DOI has,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: we believe budget independence is the best way to ensure that we're funded properly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just going to the savings portion, so looking back to previous investigations the department has done, can you provide an example of how DOI investigations have saved money for the city?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: As I mentioned, in fiscal year 26, to date, we collected $6 million in restitution for the city.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And as an example, in October 2025, a defendant was ordered to pay $1.6 million

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: in restitution after he inflated invoices and overbilled the city in contracts relating to repairing and maintaining fire alarm systems.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So that is just one specific example of how we bring back money to the city, and that goes into the city's general fund.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In other non-quantifiable ways, I think we also save the city money by deterring corruption through our oversight.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And the asylum seeker monitorship is one way where we actually are able to quantify it.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And there we identified about $5 million in mistaken overbilling that the city was able to recoup.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so $5 million in the asylum seeker.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: contracts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Interesting.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sure there's a lot of others that you can give us a list of.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I mean, if you can also provide other examples, that would be great in terms of follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Yes, we can certainly get back to that.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks which vacant positions are being removed. Shahada explains nine previously funded vacancies must be cut, including critical executive-level roles: acting general counsel, acting chief of investigations, and a departed deputy commissioner for compliance who also served as EEO officer.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on vacant positions being removed in executive plan

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I think it's important to note, because this is exactly why these positions are important, so I'm just trying to understand some of the headcount reductions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you also produce a list of titles you believe work to accomplish savings for the city and work with the administration on exemptions as well?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Yes, in response to that, I would say I believe the entire investigative division of DOI is revenue generating in the sense that

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: these are the people conducting these investigations where we get restitution and forfeiture for the city and also working to prevent

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: corruption through our policy and procedure recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And that includes confidential investigators, auditors, financial analysts, and so forth.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then moving into headcount reduction, executive plan included a headcount reduction of 11 positions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What vacant positions are being removed in this plan?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So if nothing changes and we're required to move forward with cutting 11 vacancies,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: a few of those will be vacancies that are already defunded, but it will require us to cut nine funded vacancies or previously funded vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: This presents some very difficult decisions on my part because we currently have, for example, three important vacancies at the executive level.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We currently are operating with an acting general counsel, an acting chief of investigations, and we've also recently...

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The person who was filling the role of director, sorry, deputy commissioner for compliance and also served as our EEO officer recently left.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So that is also a vacant position.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can I just ask a clarifying question?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So when you said nine funded vacancies, those are meaning

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: positions that they're currently staffing in or those are they're vacant but but were previous before the executive budget

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: came out were funded positions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And so we won't be able to hire for nine positions.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We're currently trying to maneuver, and we have various scenarios of how we would do that.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But if we were required to go forward, that may, for example, include simply not having a

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: deputy commissioner for compliance and kind of putting all their that person's responsibilities in other areas as current as is currently being done temporarily now, but that's not obviously a decision that I would like to have to make

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: and hopeful that we can avoid that right adopted budget and how

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, I know the answer, but if you could give more details in terms of how this impact, you know, what kind of impact this is going to have on the investigations that you're conducting.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then I have a follow-up question after that.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how the cuts will impact ongoing investigations. Shahada praises staff dedication but warns that years of doing more with less has reached a tipping point where triaging is no longer sufficient and DOI may have to decide which investigations it can and cannot open.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on impact of headcount cuts on ongoing investigations

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I'll start by saying since I've been at DOI,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: the past six weeks, I have been incredibly impressed by the hard work and dedication of every single staff member I've met with.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That being said, for years they have been asked to do more with less, and that takes a toll on morale and just, frankly, the ability to

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: make sure you're properly staffing every investigation you're working on.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: They've done an amazing job, but at a certain point,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: you get to a point where you have to make your triaging no longer is sufficient and you have to make decisions about what investigations you can and cannot open.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about a plan B if positions aren't restored. Shahada describes presenting cases to prosecutors early to maximize shared resources, working with state and federal law enforcement partners, and utilizing on-loan staff governed by MOUs, but warns difficult operational decisions will be required if cuts proceed.
chapters.title: Chair Lee asks about plan B for staffing and leveraging other agencies

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I guess to that point, I mean, obviously we would want to see a restoration in a lot of the positions in the budget, but

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Let's just say that doesn't end up happening, unfortunately.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What is the plan B that you have?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are you leveraging staff from other agencies to assist with the investigations?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And have those conversations started?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Yeah, so we always, as a general practice,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In our investigations, one of the things we do is present cases to prosecutors'offices for our criminal cases early so that we are maximizing the resources we have available.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Generally, those prosecutors'offices also have investigators, and we're working with law enforcement partners at the state and federal level.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And so that is something we...

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Are doing now and will continue to do.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We have on-loan staff from other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The number of those is generally governed by a memorandum of understanding with those agencies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And so we have those in place and those are certainly very helpful.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But if we are required to make these cuts, then we are going to be required to make some very difficult decisions that I am concerned will affect core operations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And obviously, on the council side, when we've been talking...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: about savings, we want to make very clear that this is not something that we want to see as a negative when it comes to actually doing the work and providing services.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so this is helpful to know.

chapters.summary: Lee asks if $6M is sufficient for stabilization. Shahada confirms it covers avoiding nine vacancy cuts, reducing forfeiture reliance, and hiring five entry-level investigators. Lee then asks about the total lease cost; Shahada reports approximately $18.5M, consuming the vast majority of city-funded OTPS alongside one integrity monitorship, leaving only a few hundred thousand dollars to operate the agency.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on $6M stabilization sufficiency and lease costs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the $6 million to stabilize the budget for the next fiscal year, is that sufficient?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What's the ideal number?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That's the stabilization number.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We are cognizant of the financial and budgetary condition that the city as a whole is facing, and so we've really tried to focus on what is necessary to keep our core operations going and not to ask for more than that at this time.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And so that's where we have come up with the $6 million number.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It would allow us to not have to defund nine vacancies and also

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: start to get on a path where we're not over relying on forfeiture funds for our basic operations as well as You know a minimal request for five entry-level confidential investigators to

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Start helping stabilize our investigative squads

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just lastly, before I pass it to the chair, in terms of the lease,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know that you said that the move saved approximately $157,700 annually, but what's the total?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Portion of the department's budget for the lease.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you update us on that cost?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Yeah, I don't know the exact percentage because I'm not very good at math, but it's about $18.5 million is the number on the lease.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So it's the vast majority of our OTPs, city tax levy OTPs is spent on the lease and one integrity monitorship for the next year.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: After that, I think we have about just a few hundred thousand dollars left

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: to operate the agency, which is why we have had to repurpose forfeiture funds to allow us to fulfill our mission.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about centralizing IT and technology needs with other agencies. Shahada notes independence requirements limit sharing but is open to exploring options. Lee then asks how the lease being included in the peg impacts other budget areas; Shahada explains the lease is not excluded from the savings target, making the peg much more difficult since it's DOI's largest OTPS item.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on OTPS centralization and lease impact on budget pegs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then are there areas like with technology, IT needs, all of those things in the OTPS lines that you would be able to centralize with the admin in terms of using, you know,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: leveraging with other agencies to save costs there for your department?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sharing of, I guess, resources.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I would have to get back to you on some specifics on that.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I know that there is, because of our independent oversight mandate, there is some aspects of our work that we need to make sure are not accessible to other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But certainly where there's an ability to do that, we're happy to look into that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And lastly, are the costs for the lease separated out when coming up with the dollar value of expected savings?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I'm sorry, could you rephrase the question?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So let me, so basically how has the lease being included in your budget impact sort of the other areas that you would potentially be able to use it for?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Well, the lease is part of the peg.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And so that actually,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: makes the peg much more difficult for us because the lease is not excluded from it and that is the largest part of our OTPS budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Interesting.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to pass it to Council Member Krishnan, Chair Krishnan.

chapters.summary: Krishnan agrees with Lee on the need for charter reform for DOI budget independence, then confirms the $6M request is purely for stabilization—not new programs—and verifies the executive budget is $2.1M less than FY26 and $12.8M less in baseline funding than prior years.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on $6M stabilization clarification and funding independence

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: There were just two points before I start my questions that I wanted to really agree with you on.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I think one is it is way past time that the city properly fund DOI so that the funds don't run out and you all can do your mission.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I agree with that completely.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I also agree that there is a fundamental...

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Conflict between in

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: agency tasked with the very independent mission being dependent on a city budget and budget process that will vary year to year, mayor to mayor.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So I fully support looking at ways that we can going forward reform the charter to make sure you all as an independent agency have an independent funding process too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Chair Lee hit a number of the points and questions, but I wanted to clarify a few things because there's a lot of information, and I wanted to really just underscore the need, the critical need for funding that you all have.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So it's my understanding, Commissioner, right, that you all need $6 million in funding in this year's budget.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Just to stabilize.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: That's not new needs, that's not new programs.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It's $6 million just to stabilize current operations and current staff on payroll.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It's just under $6 million.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Included within that is five new investigators, entry-level investigators, but that's really to stabilize things.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Currently.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And currently, the executive budget, I think you mentioned, has $2.1 million less than last year's budget.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Is that correct?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Yes, I believe so.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And $12.8 million less in baseline funding than going back several years at this point, right, taking into account the pegs from the Adams years and everything.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: That makes sense.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Krishnan confirms DOI-funded headcount dropped from 365 four years ago to 275 under the Adams administration. Shahada reveals that after paying for the lease and one integrity monitorship, DOI has only $175K-$400K remaining to run all operations before tapping forfeiture funds.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on headcount decline from 365 to 275 and remaining operating funds

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And then in total, really what we're talking about for DOI is you all have

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: A staff of 275 positions at this point, which is down from about 365 four years ago.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In DOI-funded lines, yes.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: DOI-funded lines from 365 four years ago to 275 over the course of the Adams administration as a result of the PEGs over those years.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And is it your testimony that after DOI funds its basic needs, including this lease that we have as well,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: how much exactly are you all left over with if you don't include asset forfeiture funds?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So historically, I think it's been about $175,000 to $200,000.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I think for fiscal year 26, it's a little bit more than that.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I believe it's $400,000 because we did get some additional money for lease obligations and I think moving CCPC.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: To our headquarters, but a few hundred thousand dollars.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So just to be clear, after all the fundamental basic needs are taken care of, the agency only has between 175,000, give or take, to at most 400,000 to run all of its operations?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And how much are you all planning to draw down from asset forfeiture funding for fiscal 26 and fiscal 27 to supplement but also support core operations, given the very little funding you all have left over?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So we anticipate for fiscal 2026, it'll be approximately $11 million.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We have $3 million.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Currently dedicated as a forfeiture spend for fiscal year 2027, but we expect that to go up, and particularly if we don't receive additional city tax levy OTPS funds.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks when forfeiture funds will run out. Shahada estimates 2-3 years at the current spend rate, after which DOI would be left with only a few hundred thousand dollars to run the entire agency, with no forfeiture supplement available.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on forfeiture fund depletion timeline of 2-3 years

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And if that's the case, at what point do you foresee the asset forfeiture funding running out completely?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Because as I understand it, it's coming from one specific case from...

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: many years ago?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: If we continue with the current rate of spend, it's approximately two years at the very outset three years, but in the two to three year range.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So in two years, DOI would not have asset forfeiture funding to supplement and support its core operations?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Based on the current amount of asset forfeiture funds we have, yes.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And all you'd have left at that point is the several hundred thousand, assuming nothing changes, all you'd have left to run an entire agency in two years would be the several hundred thousand dollars in funding that you have left after fundamental payments like rent and things like that.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Am I right?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That's correct.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks for the total number of positions affected by cost-saving mandates. Shahada confirms nine funded vacancies must be cut, including critical executive leadership and the IG for NYPD, but notes the impact extends agency-wide as remaining staff face higher caseloads with fewer resources.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on total positions affected by cost-saving mandates

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So another, you testified as well about given the current cost savings mandates and you spoke about the vacancy positions and other positions.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Can you just give a total amount of with the cost saving mandates in place and the

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: pegs over the years that have happened and the cuts that have happened.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: What, in total, how many positions are going to be affected by the efforts to meet the cost-saving mandates in place?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: You broke it up over nine positions, vacancies, but I'm just looking for a total number of how many positions would be affected in one way or another.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Well, we'd have to cut nine.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So currently, before the executive budget came out, there were nine positions that we would have been able to fill that we will now have to cut.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And so...

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: As I mentioned, this is particularly challenging right now because of the vacant positions we have are some critical positions in executive leadership, in very important IG positions, including the IG for the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That we are trying to figure out how we will manage if we don't receive additional funds.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So nine positions affected, and then if you all don't get the $6 million to stabilize,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: how many positions overall would be affected?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Is it just the nine, or is it other?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I think you mentioned as well there would be potential cuts to salaries of executive positions.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I mean, what are we talking about overall is the impact of not getting the funding that you all need?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Well, I think what we would do is the idea of lowering the salaries for certain positions is in order to try to minimize the number of vacant positions we have to cut and yet still meet the number, the target number.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I don't have a precise number for you of how many positions we would have to do.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We are kind of going through different scenarios, but we are, I will say we are hopeful based on our discussions with City Hall

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: that we won't have to make some of these decisions because we are hopeful that additional funding will be provided in the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I want to get to that in a second too, but the point I'm trying to make is it seems to me that it's beyond the nine positions, that nine position vacancies that would be affected.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It seems to me in order to avoid losing those positions, a number of other positions and those salary lines could potentially be affected too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Am I right?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Potentially, yes.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And I would say the whole agency would be affected because we would be asking our...

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Dedicated and talented staff to be again doing more with less and working on higher caseloads and and just being required to

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: you know, conduct more investigations with fewer staff.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And that's kind of what I was getting at, too.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It seems to me the whole agency, in one form or another, would be affected.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks about conversations with OMB and the mayor. Shahada reports she has spoken plainly about DOI's situation, believes City Hall understands the importance of DOI's mandate and the budget challenges, and is confident additional funding will be addressed in the adopted budget, though she has no specific numbers to share.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on conversations with OMB and mayor about DOI funding

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So now, at this point, how have the conversations been?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I think you indicated there's been some positive conversations, but how have they been with OMB and the mayor so far?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: To the extent you can share any sense of what trajectory you might be looking at from the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So I don't have any specific numbers to share with you.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: What I can say is that I'm confident that the mayor's office understands the situation we're in.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I've spoken plainly about it here at the council, and I've spoken about it in the same way with the mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I believe they understand the importance of DOI's mandate and mission and the challenges we are facing.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And I'm confident that they will address it in the adopted budget, but I don't have any specifics to share.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks about ideal headcount. Shahada describes a prior planning exercise projecting 585 DOI-funded positions as optimal—412 investigative, 98 operations, 48 executive and training. She notes current headcount is roughly half that level, and if new needs are approved, DOI would reach 318 funded positions plus 142 on-loan for a total of 460.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on ideal headcount of 585 vs current 275

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And so we're looking at right now a headcount of 275 down from 365 before.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: What do you believe would be the appropriate headcount and budget needed for your organization to run seamlessly?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Well, to run seamlessly, look, I'm happy to talk a little bit about

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: a process that the agency underwent under the prior commissioner, where we really, as part of the work

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: to provide a proposal to the commission to strengthen local democracy on the charter revision proposals,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That included a minimum budget for DOI, and as part of that process, DOI went through a planning exercise to estimate an optimal staffing level for the department, going unit by unit to project an ideal staffing number.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And the projected ideal for investigative staff was 412 positions,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: for operations staff, 98 positions, and executive and training staff, 48 positions, which would bring the total DOI-funded headcount to 585.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I'm not expecting in this budget landscape that that is something that we could realistically expect.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Again, I'm focused now on stabilization.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And if our new needs request is approved for the adopted budget, our core headcount on DOI-funded lines would be 318 DOI-funded positions.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We have, if we factor in 142 on-loan personnel from paid for by other city,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: agencies, then our total budgeted headcount would be 460 if our new needs request is accepted.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And it seems to me, wouldn't you agree that right now the current headcount at DOI is give or take about half of

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: where you all would want to be ideally, 265 versus the 580 that you indicated.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It seems like we're, given all these cuts over the years, about halfway from what you would fully need in an ideal level to fully fund the agency.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Yes, probably.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Would you agree with that?
