chapters.summary: Krishnan asks how DOI protects shared resources when MOUs are renewed. Shahada explains there is no mechanism to force agencies to maintain funding levels, and that intra-city funding hasn't kept pace with actual salaries—DOI must supplement from its own PS dollars, citing examples where H+H and DEP MOU funding falls short of actual salary costs by $28K-$43K per staffer.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on MOU stability and intercity funding shortfalls

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I think you touched upon this before, too, but when we talk about your agreements with other city agencies as you do work and share resources, when MOUs are up for renewal, how do you make sure that shared resources are stable and not reduced by agency savings exercises?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Well, there's no mechanism to force agencies to provide us with a certain level of funding or to not cut their resources.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: They share with DOI as part of their savings exercise.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We rely on explaining the importance of DOI's work, our record of achievements, and the productive relationships we've fostered with the relevant agencies to make our case.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I would suggest that a more stable way to manage DOI's resources would be to provide the staffing and resources directly to DOI's budget and not have to rely on discretionary MOUs for headcount.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And have there been, I mean, are there a certain number of positions, a minimum number that other departments are obligated to fund?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Have there been reductions in the MOUs for the allowable staff that other departments are obligated to fund?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And how do you, is there no way at all to protect, there's no minimum number, no sort of, you know, requirements on other agencies to obligate them to fund certain positions or work with you all to make sure they're funded in some capacity?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So there's been one MOU where we had a reduction in headcount.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That's the MOU with DEP for staff who monitor the BT2 water project upstate.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: There was a loss of two headcount there, but that was attributable to workflow and not a budget consideration.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In other MOUs, for example, with DOC, DOHMH, or DOB, we have thankfully not seen reduced headcount.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: count, but it's worth noting that the intracity funding they give us has not kept pace with the actual salaries paid to the employees.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Therefore, in certain cases, DOI has had to supplement the intracity funding with our own PS dollars to meet the required salaries.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So to give you an example, there's one staffer funded through an H&H MOU who makes $123,000, but we only received $95,000 in intercity funds.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Another staffer on the DEP MOU makes $128,000, but we receive only $100,000.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: $85,000 from DEP.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So DOI has to make up that difference out of our own PS dollars.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks about staff retention and promotions. Shahada confirms DOI has no funding for promotions, OMB does not approve merit-based raises, and all retention efforts have been self-funded by reallocating vacancy funds. She describes a prior plan to address pay disparities among deputy inspector generals and create a promotional path for investigators, all funded by repurposing vacant position dollars.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on staff retention, promotions, and OMB approval barriers

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And a few other questions that I have too before turning it over to our colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We touched on this a bit before, but when positions are affected, whether it's vacant lines or existing positions,

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It doesn't seem to me, and as you testified to, the impacts are much broader than this, this specific number of positions on the table.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: But it could actually affect staff retention, too, more broadly speaking.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Does the department have the funding to promote staff?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Are there opportunities for promotions, increases in salaries?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: If not, are these requests you are working on to explore how to

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Achieve that goal.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: How do you think about staff retention more broadly?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So I agree with you that promotion and growth opportunities are essential for hiring and retention.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: At this point, we do not have funding to promote staff.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And it's my understanding that OMB does not approve merit-based promotions and raises.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: DOI has 25 vacancies as of May 27, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: However, we only have funding to support 16 of those vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In the past, we have made requests to OMB for additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: In the past administration, we made such requests for promotions and staff retention, and we did not receive any funding from OMB.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: All of our retention efforts that DOI has made have been self-funded by reallocating resources from vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And in the last administration, even though OMB did not allocate new funding for these efforts, the approval to be able to use our existing funding still took over a year to receive.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We have, several years ago, DOI determined that our significant attrition in our investigative ranks was largely due to an absence of a path to promotion and salaries that are not competitive with other public sector

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: opportunities or the private sector.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And as a result, DOI implemented a plan that gave raises and promotions to individual confidential investigators based on merit to appropriately reflect each of their levels of experience, responsibility, and performance, and

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: address pay disparity among our deputy inspector generals.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: All of whom generally did the same type of work, had similar expertise, and perform at comparable levels, but were paid at different salaries.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And we created a clear promotional path for investigators.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But again, this was, in order to do that, we had to repurpose funds from vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So we did not receive additional funding for that.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But clearly it's an issue, and we've tried to address it, but it's going to continue to be an issue, and we don't have additional funding to continue to be able to address it.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It's very concerning.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I think, you know, for lack of a better word, it seems like the agency is robbing Peter to pay Paul.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: In other words, using pulling from one area to keep staff and pay for increased salary needs in the other.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Isn't that the case?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I think that's a good way of putting it.

chapters.summary: Krishnan notes the agency is 'robbing Peter to pay Paul.' Shahada describes DOI's in-house IOTA training program that certifies investigators as peace officers, but warns that trained investigators are routinely poached by federal and state agencies after a short time because DOI cannot offer competitive salaries or promotions.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on IOTA training program and losing trained investigators to other agencies

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: What does OMB approve?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: They're not approved.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: merit-based promotions or pay increases.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So what are they approving?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It sounds like just the transfer of money from one part of the agency to another to fund some of these initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That's, I believe, what happened in the past administration, that DOI sought approval for promotions and raises,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: was not provided that approval, and then sought approval to use funding for vacancies, for certain existing vacancies, to repurpose that to use it for promotions and raises.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Wow.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And I think the point is well made, too, that it does not allow the agency to remain competitive.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: These are positions that are, you know, you're competing with many other parts of the legal sector and otherwise and investigative sector.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It really raises serious questions about how you all can stay competitive if you can't pay well or you can't promote your staff.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Just on that note, I would add, in an effort to provide training opportunities for our investigative staff, DOI developed in-house an investigative officer training program known as IOTA.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And it allows investigators to become certified peace officers.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It trains them up on investigative procedures and tools.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And it's been a very successful program that has resulted in highly trained entry-level investigators once they've gone through that program.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And because we are not able to fund raises and promotions, what we often find is that these investigators that we've spent time and effort training are then

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: going after a short amount of time.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And being hired by other federal and state agencies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And so that's also a challenge where we're investing appropriately in ensuring we have a staff capable of conducting the important investigations that we work on.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But because of our funding issues, we're often not able to keep that staff long term.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Right, right.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks whether funding cuts will affect DOI's public reports, citing the helpful sanctuary compliance report. Shahada affirms public reports are integral to transparency and she intends to prioritize them, but acknowledges they are time-intensive and budget cuts reduce staff capacity to produce them.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on impact of funding cuts on public reports

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And my last two questions are.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: DOI publishes excellent reports that uncover a lot of issues when it comes to corruption or accountability or compliance with the laws that we have.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: As we had a hearing earlier this year, your sanctuary report on the compliance of city agencies with sanctuary laws and YPD and DOC was very helpful.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I imagine that you coming in as a new commissioner will want to continue publishing reports, publish more reports perhaps.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Do you see that being affected by the lack of funding?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: If funding continues at this level and you don't receive an increase, do you all see an ability to be able to continue to increase the number of the amount of reporting you all are doing?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Well, first, I agree that public reports are incredibly important, and I think overall transparency by DOI is incredibly important.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I think it's integral for us to be letting the public know what it is we're working on,

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: what our investigations have found, and to make policy and procedure recommendations to improve the functioning of city government.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I do intend to prioritize the publication of public reports, but as with everything else DOI does, it is affected by the budget and the funding we receive.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Public reports in particular are time-intensive efforts.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And when our staff is cut, again, that provides less staff to work on everything, including issuing public reports.

chapters.summary: Krishnan asks what happens if the $4.5M for the 9/11 investigation isn't funded. Shahada reports the vendor's best and final offer is $4M, which DOI has requested in new needs, but without that funding DOI cannot conduct the investigation at all. Krishnan also asks about public-private partnerships; Shahada says she'll need to look into it.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan on $4M needed for 9/11 investigation and public-private partnership question

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And my final question, and I'm sure Councilman Abreu will ask more about this too, but we've got a budget request for about $4.5 million for the 9-11 investigation.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: If that money is not in the budget, do you all see your ability to, will you be able to complete the report?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Will you be able to finish the investigation or even hire the outside vendor you had in mind?

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: What will happen if that money is not in the budget for this critical investigation?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So first of all, the good news is I think we can do it for a little bit less.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We can do it for $4 million.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: That's the best and final offer we received from the vendor.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So that is what we've requested in our new needs for the adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It's $4 million.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But without that $4 million.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We'll try to get you the $4.5 million anyway.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: You need the money for other stuff too.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We'll take it.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But without that money, we will not be able to conduct that investigation.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And we would like to receive that money so we can get to work.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Sorry, just really quickly following up on that.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Is there a way to, I don't know what the legal parameters are around this, but is there a way to do public-private partnerships, either with other law firms, have MOUs in place to be creative when thinking through the cost?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I'll have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I don't know the answer off the top of my head.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, followed by Councilmember Joann.

chapters.summary: Brewer raises FIFA as an example of fast money going out the door where DOI oversight is needed. Shahada agrees expedited spending carries the highest corruption risk. Brewer then asks about the NYPD IG; Shahada confirms the position has been posted, resumes are being reviewed, and interviews will begin shortly.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer on FIFA spending oversight and NYPD IG hiring

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't trust the other law firms, I'll just tell you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: First of all, thank you, and thank you for your testimony, because most people don't say they need more money, and we appreciate your honesty.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I feel very strongly, as I think my colleagues do, that it's important, and I want to thank the speaker and everybody who made today's hearing possible for that reason.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Just before we get to the many questions about the funding for what I would call the resolution for 9-11, but also, you know, things like FIFA are coming up.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Now, that's going to be fast money out the door.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That would be something where you'd be looking at that as an example of something where you need more staff to pay attention.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: You know, we're always looking at any time the city is spending money on an expedited basis or on an emergency basis.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Those are always areas where the risk of corruption and waste is often the highest.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And so those are areas we do look at.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, FIFA.

chapters.summary: Brewer discusses the 9/11 Health Watch Article 78 victory, noting the city rarely loses such proceedings, and asks about the unusual discovery order. Shahada won't comment on the litigation but confirms DOI has already identified where relevant records should be located across city agencies and is ready to begin the investigation once funding is secured, with an estimated two-year timeline from funding start.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer on 9/11 Article 78 proceeding, records, and investigation timeline

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then also, are you still working on NYPD monitors?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something you're still looking for a new head, or has that been?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We are in the process of hiring an IG for the NYPD.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: The position has been posted.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We have been reviewing resumes that we have received, and we will begin the interviewing process shortly on that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Congratulations.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So obviously we passed a resolution in the city council to have 9-11 information available to the public.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We couldn't find any other way except this wonderful opportunity for DOI to do the work.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I said from the very beginning, no matter what we do, we need you and your team, the people you're going to hire, hopefully with this $4 million or $4.5 million, to do the work.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because the only agency they came through, as you know, was the Department of Environmental Protection.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And those boxes, I went through some of them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There was some information there, but not exactly what we were looking for.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But I appreciate that that happened.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I happen to know that those boxes only came about because the commissioner at the time said, find them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They were lost.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They couldn't put them together.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And originally people said they couldn't find them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Well, this Article 78 is very interesting.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: To the credit of the 9-11 Health Watch, they went to court.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And they won on Article 78 because, and I have great respect for the head of the law department, C.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Banks, but his FOIL people said they had no boxes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Well, we know that there are boxes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We know, for instance, that in the archives of a very special reporter who was a friend of all of ours in Texas, we found a letter that was a memo that was sent to Bob Harding.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So we know there are boxes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We know there's information.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And for whatever reason, the folks at the law department can't find anything, which I don't believe.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my understanding is that the city of New York rarely loses an Article 78 proceeding.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In fact, I don't even know there's a prior example of losing one, at least recently in Article 78.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But an order of discovery against the city of New York, in addition, regarding an Article 78, is very, very unusual.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'd love you to comment to see if I'm correct that perhaps the only way to get this information is to do the kind of work that I hope you would be able to do if you get the funding.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just was wondering if you could comment on the Article 78 and the discovery and the fact that this is the only way to get this information.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So I'm not prepared to comment on the Article 78 proceeding, but I will say that as part of its efforts to be prepared to conduct the

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: 9-11 toxins investigation once the funding is secured, that DOI

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: made inquiries of various city agencies that one would expect to have the type of records that would be relevant to the investigation, received responses from those agencies.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And so...

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: is aware of where the relevant records should be located.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And once we have the funding to begin the investigation, the process of fully accessing those records, some of which may be in systems that require technological

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: issues to be addressed because they're old systems, but to compile that information and start to go through it and start to investigate the matter that you all have asked us to.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: We are ready to get that started, and all we are waiting for is the funding.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you have any sense from the company or from past experience how long it might take to do this work?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: So I think the initial plan, had we been funded, when the council mandated us to conduct this investigation, was to complete it within two years.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I think that is the timeline we're looking at, since almost a year of that has gone by now without getting the funding.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I think we will need...

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Two years from when we get the funding, but that is the goal and I think it's achievable.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Brewer raises concerns about the FOIL system after the law department claimed no records existed despite evidence to the contrary. Shahada acknowledges the frustration from her private practice experience and suggests FOIL could be an area DOI examines, which Brewer encourages.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer on FOIL system improvements

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And also just on a sort of an additional issue, I'm concerned about the fact that the FOIL stated or the FOIL staff stated that they had no records.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you have any sense maybe from past experience why or how we could improve the FOIL system?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know there is at the Department of Records, there's now a dashboard and there's more information about FOIL in general, but it does seem to me that there needs to be a better system for FOIL.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you have any sense of how that should work?

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I don't have any recommendations right now about FOIL.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: I can tell you as when I was in private practice, I filed some FOIL, so I understand the frustration there.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: And I think that may be an area that DOI can also look into.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It's a very important area, of course.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's what I was trying to hint at, so I appreciate that you have that interest in addition.

chapters.summary: Brewer suggests the 9/11 investigation could serve as a model for DOI's capabilities in complex investigations, raises concerns about disorganized city warehouse records, and notes the council previously hired DOI-trained investigators who were excellent. She pledges to fight for the 9/11 funding and emphasizes the Article 78 win demonstrates why it's needed.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer on records management, staff retention, and support for 9/11 funding

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I also, I think that there are, you know, that this would be a wonderful example if we get the funding, which I hope we will, to state that

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: the Department of Investigation has full capability of doing these, what I would probably be challenging and complicated investigations.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This would be an example.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And that this is the way that we should be looked at for the future.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There's not only waste and fraud, there's also just better government.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have a feeling, I could be wrong, that there are a whole bunch of boxes and records around the city that are in different warehouses that need to have some kind of organizational

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: improvement, shall we say.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I'm hoping that in addition to this, that there is that also comes about.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I've been in several warehouses recently, and oh my goodness, nobody knows what's exactly there.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I think this would be a good example to try to use for others.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then just finally, I just want to agree with my colleagues saying you need the funding to be able to do the work.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That you have been assigned.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Without the funding, you cannot do it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have to say that we did, some years ago, steal some of your staff who came to the city council as investigators, and they're fabulous, and attorneys, and they're fabulous, and you train them very well, or your agency trained them very well.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So you do need to retain, that's a complicated,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: job to be an investigator and to do it well.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That was so important.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'm just here to say we're all going to fight for the money for this particular 9-11.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think that the fact that, you know, the Article 78 was won is a perfect example of why we need to do it, because obviously the city has not done their job in terms of responding to the FOIL.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's a huge win for the 9-11 Health Watch.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much, and I look forward to...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Making sure that this information is available to the public.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Councilmember Joy?

chapters.summary: Ariola raises concerns about NYC spending approximately $81K per person on homeless services, citing a lobbyist who described payments to developers, and asks whether DOI is investigating potential corruption in homeless services contracting. Shahada confirms DOI has historically investigated shelter-related issues, issued public reports with recommendations, and will continue to look at the area.
chapters.title: Council Member Ariola on homeless services spending and corruption concerns

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, and very nice to meet you, Commissioner, first time.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And I think DOI's job is very important, and also the money cities spend on DOI should be able to recoup from corruptions.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: For New York City has been, I see the report before, New York City spent roughly 81K per person on homeless services last year.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And are you guys doing any investigation on this issue?

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It really needs that money.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And I do see some developer.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: They are trying to make money from most vulnerable community.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: There's a developer, a lobbyist personally come to me and saying, well, we give so-and-so

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: this much of money.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I see there's corruption.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And a lot of people in New York City have a question mark in their head.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It's why the city spent so much money on every single homeless person.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: We want to help a homeless person, but why those...

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Has such $81,000 a year, it's a median income,

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: house family income a year.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: So it doesn't make sense to most of people how much the city spend the money, how do they spend the money, is there any corruption there, is those homeless people really get helped.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: I just want to get your attention on this issue because it's a lot of

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: people's concern.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Thank you for raising that.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: It's certainly an area where historically we have investigated and put resources to and issued public reports including on

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: some of the issues surrounding shelters and issued recommendations on how to improve in those areas.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: But it's certainly something we've looked at and will continue to look at.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: And the public really needs to trust your work because it's not your appointment.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It's not only from the mayor.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: It's also public trust.

utterances.speaker: Nadia Shihata
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Joann Ariola
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Chair Krishnan thanks Commissioner Shahada and her team for testifying, and concludes the DOI budget hearing and the day's hearings.
chapters.title: Chair Krishnan closes DOI budget hearing

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I think with that, it concludes actually our budget hearing with DOI and for the day, actually.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So I want to thank you so much, Commissioner, for coming out to testify and for your team being here.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: So thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: And that concludes our hearing for today.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

summary: NYC Council holds FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for the Department of Investigation, with Commissioner Nadia Shahada testifying in her sixth week on the job about DOI's severe budget crisis.

Commissioner Shahada requests $6M to stabilize DOI operations and $4M for the council-mandated 9/11 toxins investigation, warning that headcount has dropped from 588 to 444 since FY2020 and forfeiture funds will run dry in 2-3 years.

Council members Lee, Krishnan, Brewer, and Ariola press Shahada on budget cuts, staff retention challenges, forfeiture fund sustainability, the 9/11 investigation timeline, and homeless services oversight, with broad agreement that DOI needs more funding and budgetary independence.
title: Committee on Oversight and Investigations
